title: How to Turn a Discovery Call Transcript Into Your Next Actions
metaTitle: Turn Discovery Call Transcripts Into Next Actions
metaDescription: Use AI to turn a discovery call transcript into a focused summary, follow-up plan, and next steps you can actually complete.
excerpt: A discovery call transcript is only useful when it leads to a clear next step. Here is a simple AI workflow for turning a messy conversation into organized notes, follow-up, and a short task list.
author: Krista Mashore

featuredImage.alt: Notebook and laptop showing a discovery call action plan

faq.question: What should I give AI besides the call transcript?
faq.answer: Give it the purpose of the call, your definition of a qualified opportunity, and any follow-up rules you already use. Do not add information that was not discussed or verified.

faq.question: Can AI decide whether I should take on a client?
faq.answer: AI can organize the evidence and point out open questions, but you should make the decision. Review the summary against the actual conversation before acting on it.

faq.question: How long should the final action list be?
faq.answer: Keep the immediate list short. Three to five actions for the next few days is usually easier to review and finish than a long list of every possible task.

faq.question: How do I keep an AI follow-up email from sounding generic?
faq.answer: Ask AI to use only details from the conversation, name the client's stated priority, and leave out broad promises. Then edit the draft until it sounds like something you would actually send.

faq.question: What if my transcript contains sensitive information?
faq.answer: Follow your professional and company rules before placing a transcript into an AI tool. Remove information you do not need for the task and check the tool's current privacy terms yourself.

body:
Use AI to turn the transcript into four things: what the person wants, what you promised, what is still unknown, and the next actions with owners and dates. That gives you a useful record of the conversation instead of another block of text you may never open again.

This workflow works for a discovery call with a coaching prospect, a seller, a borrower, a consulting lead, or a potential partner. The goal is not to have AI make the relationship feel automated. The goal is to remove the clerical work between a good conversation and a thoughtful follow-up.

Start with the outcome you need

Before you paste a transcript into an AI tool, decide what you want back. A vague request such as “summarize this call” often produces a vague summary. Ask for a decision-support document instead.

Your request can be as simple as this:

“Read this discovery call transcript and create a practical follow-up plan. Separate the prospect's stated goals, concerns, current situation, decision process, commitments I made, open questions, and next actions. Use only information in the transcript. Mark anything uncertain as unknown. Do not invent details.”

That last instruction matters. A polished paragraph can still be wrong. You want a clear boundary between what was said, what you can reasonably infer, and what needs to be confirmed.

The four-part transcript workflow

1. Pull out the person's actual priorities

Ask AI to list the person's goals in their own language. Then ask it to separate a desired outcome from a surface request. Someone might ask about content, for example, while describing a larger problem with inconsistent leads or an overloaded calendar.

Do not turn every comment into a business need. Have AI label each item as a stated goal, concern, constraint, or background detail. This keeps you from treating a casual remark as a promise or a buying signal.

For a real estate conversation, the output might include a target move date, a concern about timing, and a question about preparation. For a consultant, it might include a team bottleneck, a desired result, and a limit on available hours. The exact categories matter less than keeping them visible and separate.

2. Create a promise and question ledger

The second pass should focus on the parts of the conversation that create responsibility. Ask AI to list every commitment you made, every item the other person agreed to send, and every question that remains unanswered.

  • Commitments you made
  • Items the prospect agreed to provide
  • Questions you need to answer
  • Decisions that still belong to the prospect
  • Statements that require confirmation

This is where many follow-ups go wrong. The email sounds warm, but it forgets the document you said you would send or quietly assumes that the other person is ready to buy. A promise ledger gives you a simple review point before you send anything.

3. Turn the ledger into a small action plan

Next, ask for actions, not themes. Each action should have one owner, one verb, and a suggested due date based only on the timing discussed in the call. If no timing was discussed, say “timing to confirm” rather than making one up.

  1. Action: Send the preparation checklist. Owner: Me. Timing: Before the next call.
  2. Action: Confirm the decision timeline. Owner: Prospect. Timing: Unknown.
  3. Action: Answer the question about the service process. Owner: Me. Timing: In the follow-up.

Keep the immediate list to three to five actions. Put longer-term ideas in a separate section called “later possibilities.” This prevents a useful call from turning into a task pile that makes the next step less likely to happen.

4. Draft a personal follow-up, then edit it yourself

Only after the first three passes should you ask for a follow-up draft. Give AI the specific points that need to appear: what you heard, what you are sending, what you need from the other person, and the proposed next step.

Use a prompt like this:

“Write a short follow-up email based only on the approved notes below. Open by reflecting the person's main priority in plain language. Include the two commitments I made, one question I need answered, and one clear next step. Do not add enthusiasm, claims, urgency, or information that is not in the notes. Keep it conversational.”

Then edit the draft. Remove anything that sounds unlike you. Replace broad phrases with the person's actual words when they fit. Check every date, name, promise, and question against the transcript. AI can give you a strong first draft, but you are still responsible for the relationship and the accuracy of the message.

Use a consistent record after every call

The real benefit appears when each conversation ends in the same place. Save a short record with five sections:

  • Purpose: Why the call happened.
  • Priority: What the person most wants to solve.
  • Evidence: Relevant facts they stated.
  • Open loop: What needs confirmation.
  • Next step: Who does what and when.

You can store this record in the place where you already manage contacts and tasks. The tool matters less than the habit. If your notes live in one place, your task list in another, and your follow-up draft in a third, the process will still feel heavy.

Set a review rule for yourself: no call is complete until the record has been checked, the follow-up has been edited, and the next action has an owner. That rule turns the transcript into part of your operating rhythm.

Three checks before you trust the output

First, compare the stated priorities with the transcript. Did AI capture what mattered, or did it give extra weight to a minor detail? Second, inspect every claim and commitment. If you cannot point to the source in the conversation, label it as a question or remove it. Third, read the email aloud. If you would not say it to the person, rewrite it.

Also check your handling rules before using any transcript. A call may contain financial, health, household, or other private information. Use the least amount of information needed for the task, and follow the requirements that apply to your business.

Make the workflow sound like you

A transcript workflow should save time without flattening your voice. Keep a short instruction that describes how you communicate: whether you prefer brief emails, direct questions, warm openings, or clear choices. Add two examples of follow-ups you are proud to send, with private details removed.

That reference gives AI a better target than a request to “sound professional.” It also gives you a repeatable way to spot when the draft has drifted. You can read more about checking whether an AI system sounds like you in the You-Replacement Test, and about follow-up writing in the three-pass email method.

One good discovery call should produce clarity, not a second administrative job. Use AI for the sorting, labeling, and first draft. Keep the judgment, final edits, and human connection with you. When every call ends with a verified record and one clear next step, your business becomes easier to run one conversation at a time.

If you want a larger set of practical workflows for creating authority and running your business, learn the AI system.

title: Past-Client AI Nurture: The 12-Month Touch System That Brings Referrals Back
metaDescription: The 12-month past-client touch schedule that brings referrals back. What AI handles, what stays human, and the exact stack to run it.
excerpt: Most agents drop their past clients within 90 days of closing. Here's the 12-month touch system that brings them back, AI-assisted where it should be.
author: Krista Mashore

featuredImage.alt: Past-client AI nurture 12-month touch system for real estate agents

faq.question: How many past clients do I need before this is worth doing?
faq.answer: Even 20. The system works at any volume. If you have 20 past clients and one refers a deal this year, you've covered the cost of your whole stack for two years.

faq.question: What if I haven't talked to my past clients in years?
faq.answer: Start with a re-introduction email. It's been a while, here's what's been going on in your zip code since you bought, and I'd love to catch up. Then begin the 12-month schedule from there.

faq.question: Can AI write the phone call script for Month 5?
faq.answer: It can draft a script. Do not read it. Use it as a memory jog. The call has to feel like you, not a telemarketer.

faq.question: What CRM should I pick?
faq.answer: Whatever you'll actually use. I see Follow Up Boss most often with coaching clients, but kvCORE and Sierra Interactive both work. The CRM matters less than whether you log in.

faq.question: How do I keep AI from making everything sound the same?
faq.answer: Build a brand voice doc and load it into a Claude Project. Then every draft runs through your voice, not generic AI voice. Without this step, your past clients will feel the tone change immediately.

faq.question: Do I really need a video tip every month?
faq.answer: No. One a quarter is fine if monthly is too much. The 12-touch schedule is the floor, not the ceiling. Some months you'll have something better to send.

body:
You sold a couple a house in March of 2023. Beautiful closing, hugs in the driveway, you sent them a charcuterie board, and then you got busy with the next deal. It's now mid-2026. They just listed with someone else. You found out from Instagram. You felt sick about it for two days.

This happens to good agents every month and it's almost always the same problem. You closed the deal, you moved on, and somebody else stayed in their inbox for three years while you didn't.

The fix is a 12-month touch system. AI handles most of it. The two or three touches that matter most stay human. Below is the schedule I run with my coaching clients and the exact tools that make it actually possible.

Why past clients matter more than cold leads

The Profile of Home Buyers and Sellers has shown for years that a large share of sellers either use an agent they've worked with before or were referred by someone in their network. The most recent figures I've seen put repeat-and-referral business well over half of total transactions for established agents, with top agents often citing 70 to most their pipeline coming from past clients and the people those clients send. You should pull the current numbers directly from the official research page before quoting them publicly: see for the latest Profile of Home Buyers and Sellers.

The point is this. Past clients are the highest-ROI asset you own. They already trust you. They've already paid you. They know how you work. And they're the cheapest, fastest source of the next deal, if you stay in front of them.

The problem isn't that agents don't believe this. The problem is that staying in front of 200 past clients for 12 months is a lot of work and most agents quit by month three. AI changes that math.

The 12-Month Touch Schedule

Twelve touches over twelve months. Some are automated, some are AI-assisted, some are pure human. Here's the schedule.

Month 1 (closing month). Closing gift drop. HUMAN. Don't outsource this. Walk it over yourself if they're local. Mail it with a handwritten card if they're not. AI cannot do this and it shouldn't.

Month 2. "How's the house?" check-in text. HUMAN, AI-drafted. A real text from your phone. Have Claude draft it based on what you remember from the closing (kid's name, the dog, the kitchen remodel they were planning). Then rewrite it in your own thumbs. Send.

Month 3. Market update email. AI. A short note. "Here's what's happening in your zip code since you bought." Automate this through your CRM. Tools like Follow Up Boss, kvCORE, or Sierra Interactive will pipe MLS data in. Have Claude write the human-sounding intro, not the canned market update template.

Month 4. Home anniversary "did you know" tip. AI. A short email or video. Something useful. Filter changes for HVAC. The right time to fertilize. Permit reminders if they were planning a remodel. HeyGen with your own avatar makes the video version cheap to produce.

Month 5. Phone call. HUMAN. Not a text. Not an email. Pick up the phone. AI is not allowed here. You're going to want to skip this one. Don't. This is the touch that creates referrals.

Month 6. Six-month "how's the house feeling" survey. AI-handled, human-reviewed. A 3-question email. "What's working? What's not? What would you change?" Anything they flag gets a personal reply from you, not an autoresponder. The replies are gold for the next year of content.

Month 7. Useful local content. AI-drafted, human-curated. A short roundup of what's opening in town, what's worth driving to, school district news, summer events. Local. Specific. Useful. AI drafts the structure, you add the one inside thing nobody else has.

Month 8. Birthday or move-in anniversary card. HUMAN, with AI assist. Real card. Real handwriting. Use a service like Handwrytten or Postable if you must, but write the message yourself. AI helps you remember the date, not write the words.

Month 9. Tax/financial reminder. AI. A short note about the home-related tax stuff (property tax due dates, homestead exemption if relevant, the IRS 1098 for mortgage interest if it's tax season). Useful. Not salesy.

Month 10. "Who do you know?" referral ask. HUMAN, with AI script. The direct ask. Not "if you know anyone." Specific. "Hey, do you know anybody at your office who's been talking about moving?" Claude can draft three variations for you to pick from. The send button is yours.

Month 11. Pop-by drop. HUMAN. If they're local, drop something off. Pumpkins in fall, fresh tomatoes in summer, holiday cookies in December. Cheap. Memorable. Almost nobody does this anymore so it lands hard.

Month 12. Year-in-review note plus market temperature read. AI-drafted, you review. What happened in their zip code this year. Average sale price change. Days on market. Whether the inventory shifted. Their home is probably worth a different number than it was a year ago. Give them the read.

What AI handles vs. what stays human

The line is simpler than it sounds. AI handles anything repeatable, anything data-driven, and anything that scales. Humans handle anything that requires a heartbeat.

AI handles: - Market update emails (the data layer) - Anniversary reminders firing into your calendar - Drafting the first version of every email and text - Filtering past-client replies and routing them to you - Producing the video version of any tip using your AI avatar

Humans handle: - The closing gift - The Month 5 phone call - The handwritten card moments - The actual conversation when someone replies - Anything emotional, personal, or trust-building

If you forget that line and let AI do the human touches, you'll feel the drop-off. Past clients can tell when they're being marketed to by a bot. The whole point of this system is that they don't feel marketed to at all.

The stack I'd actually use in 2026

You can run this with a small stack. You don't need 14 tools.

  • A CRM that holds your past-client list and fires off the scheduled emails. Follow Up Boss is what I see most coaching clients use. kvCORE and Sierra Interactive also work. Pick one and stick with it for two years.
  • Claude with a Project for your brand voice. Every email, text, and script draft runs through it. Costs about $20 a month. If you've never set one up, my piece on claude-projects-setting-up-first-one walks through it.
  • HeyGen for the video versions of your monthly tips. Saves the production cost. Don't overuse the AI avatar. One avatar video a month is fine. Five feels weird.
  • Handwrytten for the card touches if your handwriting is illegible. Better than skipping the card.
  • A simple spreadsheet or your CRM's task feature to track which touch each past client got. Even the best CRM lets people fall through if you don't audit monthly.

That's it. Five tools. Maybe $80 a month total if you're already on a CRM.

Why this works

It works because past clients want to hear from you and almost nobody bothers. Twelve touches a year breaks down to one a month. One a month is sustainable. AI does the heavy drafting so you're not staring at a blinking cursor at 10pm. The human touches are the ones that prove you're still real.

The agents in my Level Up program who run this system are pulling 60 to a lot of their annual transactions from past clients and referrals within 24 months of starting. That's not magic. That's the math of staying in front of 200 people for a year while everybody else forgets.

Want the full schedule and templates?

I built out every one of these 12 touches with the actual email templates, the phone call scripts, the Claude prompts, and the CRM setup steps inside Level Up. If you want the whole thing handed to you, come look at the program.

title: The 'You-Replacement Test': How to Know Your AI System Is Actually Working
metaTitle: The You-Replacement Test: Is Your AI System Working?
metaDescription: A 7-question diagnostic for coaches and consultants to check whether your AI system is saving you hours or quietly creating more cleanup work.
excerpt: Seven questions to figure out if your AI system is replacing most your work or adding cleanup hours. Run the diagnostic before you buy another tool.
author: Krista Mashore

featuredImage.alt: Seven-question diagnostic checklist for testing whether an AI system is replacing real work for a coach or consultant

faq.question: How often should I run the You-Replacement Test on my system?
faq.answer: Once a quarter is plenty. Run it more often than that and you'll be in constant tweaking mode, which is its own form of procrastination.

faq.question: My business is too unique for AI to handle. Does this still apply?
faq.answer: That's what every coach thinks until they invest in the brand voice and knowledge layers. The uniqueness of your business is exactly what gets captured in those layers. Without them, yes, generic AI is useless for you. With them, the test works.

faq.question: What's the single biggest reason systems fail this test?
faq.answer: The brand voice layer. If the AI doesn't sound like you, every output needs heavy edits, and you score zero on question five.

faq.question: Do I need a custom-built agent for this to work?
faq.answer: No. Most coaches and consultants can pass the test with Claude Projects, a brand voice Skill, and one or two workflows in an automation tool. Custom agents are for later.

faq.question: How do I know if my AI knowledge layer is good enough?
faq.answer: If your assistant could use it without texting you while you're on vacation, it's good enough. If they'd have to text you, you have gaps to fill.

faq.question: Can I run this test on a free AI plan?
faq.answer: You can run the test but you'll probably score lower because the persistence features (custom instructions, knowledge bases) on most platforms are paid. Free plans are for trying. Paid is for systems.

body:
Okay so you've got an AI system. You've spent some money. You've spent more hours than you want to count tweaking prompts and uploading documents and watching YouTube videos at 1.5x speed. And now you have this nagging feeling that maybe it's not doing what you hoped, but you don't want to admit it because you've sunk a lot into it.

I have a name for that feeling. It's called the "I'm still doing all the work" feeling. And there's a test I run on my own systems to figure out if I should keep them, fix them, or kill them. I call it the You-Replacement Test.

The premise is simple. If you handed your AI system to a smart assistant who has never met you, could that assistant produce most the work that comes out of your office without you in the room? Not 100. We're not trying to clone you. Eighty.

Here are the seven questions I run through.

Question 1: Does it sound like me on a bad day?

Not your best writing day. Your tired Tuesday writing day. The day where you cranked out an email at 4pm between calls.

Most people test their AI against their best work and then are disappointed when it doesn't match. Wrong test. Your AI should match your average day, which is the day that fills your inbox and content calendar. If the AI is too polished, it's wrong. If it's missing your weird little turns of phrase, it's wrong.

This is a Claude Projects strength, by the way. If you've fed the Project enough of your actual writing (the messy stuff, not only the published stuff), the output starts catching your rhythm. As of mid-2026, you can have persistent custom instructions plus an attached knowledge base of your own writing samples. That's the combination that gets you to "sounds like me on a bad day."

Question 2: Can someone else use it without calling me?

Imagine you go on vacation for a week. No phone. No Slack. (I know, terrifying. Stay with me.)

Your assistant or your VA opens up your AI system and tries to draft a follow-up email to a new client. Do they have everything they need? The pricing table, your standard objection responses, the way you sign off, the tone you use when someone is on the fence?

If they have to text you, the system failed. The whole point is portability of you.

Question 3: Does it stop me from re-explaining the same thing for the fifth time?

I have a coaching client, let's call her Dana. She's a financial coach with about 90 active clients. She kept telling me she "uses AI for content" but then I asked her how often she still types out her credentialing story or her three-step money framework into a new chat. Five times a week. Maybe more.

That's not a working AI system. That's an expensive autocomplete.

A real system stores the things you'd otherwise re-explain. Your origin story, your frameworks, your standard answers to objections, your case studies, your pricing logic. Once. Forever. Available to the AI on demand. If you're still copy-pasting your own bio into prompts, your knowledge layer is broken.

Question 4: Does it know when to NOT respond?

This one trips people up. Good AI systems include rules for what not to do. Don't reply to refund requests automatically. Don't draft anything political. Don't promise timelines you haven't approved. Don't quote prices that haven't been finalized.

If your AI is willing to answer anything you throw at it, that's not freedom, that's risk. The systems that scale well have guardrails written into the brand voice Skill or the Project instructions. Mine has about a dozen "do not" rules and they've saved me embarrassment more than once.

Question 5: Does the output need more than 15 minutes of editing per piece?

This is the practical one. Time it. Set an actual timer.

If a 600-word email draft needs 25 minutes of editing, your AI is not saving you time. You'd have been faster writing it yourself. The whole point of the system is to compress your editing time, not your typing time. (There's a difference. Typing was never the slow part for most of us. Decision-making and structure are the slow part.)

When my system is dialed in, an email goes from 35 minutes of writing to maybe 7 minutes of editing. That's the win. Not "I clicked a button and it published."

Question 6: Does it improve when I correct it?

Here's where I see entrepreneurs lose patience. They correct the AI once, the next output is wrong in the same way, they assume the AI is dumb, and they quit.

Wrong. The AI is doing exactly what you told it to do. If you didn't write the correction into a Skill or a Project instruction, the AI has no memory of your feedback. (That's not a bug. That's how the tools work, at least at the time I'm writing this.)

A working system has a feedback loop. Every time you correct something, you update the underlying Skill or the Project's custom instructions. The system gets sharper week over week instead of repeating the same mistakes.

Quick tangent. This is the part of AI nobody markets, because "you have to maintain it" is not a sexy selling point. But it's the truth. A garden you don't water dies.

Question 7: Does it free up your highest-value hours?

The final question, and maybe the most important. What did you do with the time the AI gave you back?

If the answer is "I'm doing the same volume of work, with more output," your AI is a treadmill. You're moving faster but going nowhere different. The point of the You-Replacement Test isn't to produce more content. It's to free up the hours you'd otherwise spend on production so you can do the things only you can do. Sales calls. Strategy. Recording the next round of source material so the system gets sharper. Going outside. Whatever.

If you can't point to a specific block of time the AI gave back to you, the system isn't replacing you. It's distracting you.

Scoring it

Run all seven on your current system. Be honest. (You don't have to tell anyone.)

Seven yesses means you have a real working system and you should stop tweaking it and go sell more.

Five or six means you have a system with one or two leaks. Fixable in a weekend.

Four or fewer means you don't have a system, you have a tool collection. That's a different problem and it requires the kind of foundational work I write about in the 5-layer AAOS piece.

What to do with the result

Don't beat yourself up if you scored low. Most people score low the first time. (I scored a three the first time I ran this on myself in early 2025. Three out of seven. Humbling.)

Then I rebuilt the brand voice layer, redocumented my knowledge layer, and the score climbed. It took me about four months of incremental work, not a heroic weekend.

If you want the full step-by-step on how to fix each of the seven failure points, that's the bulk of what I teach inside my Level Up training. It's where I walk through the exact diagnostic and the rebuild process I used on my own business. You can grab it at kristamashore.com/LevelUp.

title: The 7 AI Tools Running Krista's Content Engine Right Now
metaTitle: The 7 AI Tools Running My Content Engine
metaDescription: An honest tour of the 7 AI tools running my content engine: Claude, Descript, HeyGen, Opus Clip, Gamma, ElevenLabs, and Fathom.
excerpt: Seven AI tools run my content engine right now. Some of them earned their spot. One of them is on probation. Here's the full honest tour.
author: Krista Mashore

featuredImage.alt: Seven labeled tool icons arranged around a central content engine illustration

faq.question: Do I need all seven tools to start?
faq.answer: No. Start with Claude. Add Descript and Opus Clip when you're recording weekly. Add the rest when you have a specific need. Buying tools you don't use is the most common AI-stack mistake I see.

faq.question: What's the total monthly cost of this stack?
faq.answer: It varies a lot based on plan tiers and how heavily you use the voice and video tools. I won't quote exact numbers because pricing changes, but the full stack at production volume runs in the low to mid hundreds per month. Check each tool's current pricing page.

faq.question: Which tool would you cut if you had to?
faq.answer: Gamma. I love it for live workshops but I could survive without it. Claude is the one I'd never give up. It's the connective tissue.

faq.question: Why ElevenLabs instead of the AI voices built into Descript?
faq.answer: ElevenLabs handles emotional tone better in my testing. Descript's voice cloning has improved a lot but for branded audio I still reach for ElevenLabs.

faq.question: Is Fathom that much better than the alternatives?
faq.answer: For my workflow yes. The auto-summaries and the ability to tag moments mid-call save me a real chunk of time. Other meeting tools are fine. I just stuck with this one because it works.

faq.question: What about ChatGPT or Gemini in your stack?
faq.answer: I keep ChatGPT around for quick image generation and a few research tasks. Gemini I use sparingly. Claude does most my writing work.

body:
If you're going to copy somebody's content stack, I'd rather you copy mine with full knowledge of what each tool does and where each one falls short. Most "AI stack" articles read like sponsored ads. This one isn't sponsored. None of these companies pay me. A few of them have annoyed me this year and I'm going to tell you which ones.

There are seven tools currently running my content engine. Some of them have been in the stack for two years. One of them I'm still deciding on. Here they are, in the order they get used in a typical day.

1. Claude (writing, ideation, brand voice, everything)

Claude is the central nervous system. If something disappeared and I had to pick one tool to keep, this is the one.

What I use it for: blog drafts, email sequences, social captions, sales pages, recap emails after coaching calls, brainstorm partners, voice-checks on copy I wrote by hand, prompts for other tools, and the occasional emotional rant when I'm trying to figure out what to say about a tough topic.

Why it stays: it handles long-form voice work better than anything else I've tested. When I drop in 600 words of brand voice rules and three example emails, Claude uses them. Other models tend to nod politely and then write in their own voice. Claude listens.

What annoys me: it sometimes still slips a word I've banned from my brand voice. I have to do a final ctrl-F pass on every draft. Not a dealbreaker. Just a thing.

Cost commitment: I pay for the Pro tier. The cost is irrelevant compared to the time it saves me each week.

2. Descript (video editing, transcript editing, polish)

Descript is the second pillar. Every video I publish goes through it.

What I use it for: transcript-based video editing, removing filler words, Studio Sound noise cleanup, captions, light color correction, and quick podcast-style audio edits.

Why it stays: the transcript editing is still the killer feature. I cut a word from the text and the video cuts with it. Two years in and it still feels like magic.

What annoys me: rendering large projects can drag if you've got a lot of overlays. I learned to keep my projects lean.

I've heard rumors of competitors closing the gap. I'll switch the day someone else does it as well. Until then, Descript stays.

3. HeyGen (avatars and translated versions)

HeyGen is the tool I use the least and miss the most when I need it.

What I use it for: generating avatar versions of myself for translated content (Spanish mostly), creating short explainer videos when I don't have time to film, and producing branded video updates for partners without setting up a camera.

Why it stays: the avatar quality has gotten genuinely good. As of mid-2026 the lip-sync is close enough that casual viewers don't catch it. The motion looks more natural than it did even six months ago.

What annoys me: it's expensive at the volume I'd want to use it, and the avatar still has a faint "AI uncanny" quality if you stare at it. I treat it as a supplement, never a replacement for real video.

Honest take: if you don't need translated content or scaled avatar use, you can skip this one for now.

4. Opus Clip (the auto-clipper)

This is the tool that does the heaviest lifting per minute of my time.

What I use it for: turning long videos into short vertical clips for Reels, Shorts, TikTok, and LinkedIn.

Why it stays: it saves me hours every week. I upload a 22-minute video and within 10 to 15 minutes I have a stack of clips ready to refine.

What annoys me: the suggested "virality scores" are basically useless. Some of my best-performing clips were the ones it rated lowest. I trust my eye now, not the score.

What it does well: the auto-captions are accurate enough that I rarely have to retype lines (Opus Clip does mess up names, so I always do a captions pass before publishing).

5. Gamma (decks and visual content)

Gamma is the tool I bring out when I need a deck fast.

What I use it for: live workshop decks, sales presentations, lead-magnet PDFs that need a more designed look than a Google Doc allows.

Why it stays: I can generate a real deck in about 20 minutes that used to take me a half day. The auto-design is good enough that I rarely have to redo slides.

What annoys me: the AI-generated visuals can be too "templatey" for some of my brand work. For high-stakes decks I still hand-design key slides.

Honest take: this is the tool I'd cut if I had to. Not because it's bad. Because I use it less often than the others.

6. ElevenLabs (voice generation and cloning)

ElevenLabs is the tool that surprised me the most this year.

What I use it for: generating voiceover for video projects when I don't have time to record, creating audio versions of blog posts, and (carefully and with permission) generating branded audio for course modules.

Why it stays: the voice quality is genuinely close to a real recording. Emotional tone is the part most voice tools mess up and ElevenLabs handles it better than the others I've tested.

What annoys me: voice cloning ethics make me cautious. I only clone my own voice and I'm careful about which audio I publish using a clone. I also wouldn't trust anybody else to be careful, so if you use voice cloning, use it on yourself only and disclose when audio is AI-generated. That's a strong opinion. Disagree if you want, but that's my line.

7. Fathom (meeting AI)

Fathom is the quiet winner of the stack.

What I use it for: AI notetaking on coaching calls, Zoom meetings with my team, partner conversations, and the occasional client onboarding.

Why it stays: the auto-summaries are accurate, I can tag moments during the call with a single click, and the highlight reels make follow-up emails almost write themselves.

What annoys me: very little. Honestly. This is the most boring tool in the stack and that's a compliment.

Honest take: there are other strong contenders here. Fireflies, Otter, Read. If you're already using one of those and it works, don't switch. Tool churn is a tax on your time.

What this stack costs me in time

Total time saved per week using this stack vs. my old workflow: somewhere between 12 and 18 hours. Some weeks more. Some weeks less.

Total time spent setting up the stack initially: probably 25 hours spread over two months. Most of that was building the Claude brand-voice project and learning Descript's keyboard shortcuts.

If you set up just the first four tools (Claude, Descript, Opus Clip, Fathom), you'll get most of the return without the complexity of the full stack.

The tool you're missing isn't a tool at all

Last thought. Most people who ask me about my stack think they need more tools. They don't. They need a workflow. The tools are the muscles. The workflow is the skeleton.

You can run a serious content engine with three of these seven. You cannot run any content engine without a workflow.

Want to see the engine running?

I show the whole thing inside Level Up: the saved prompts in Claude, the Descript shortcuts I use most, the Opus Clip settings that work for me, and the Fathom workflow for turning a call into next week's social posts. We sit down together and I walk you through it. Come meet me at Level Up and I'll show you the engine in real time.

title: MCP Servers Explained: The 5 to Set Up First (For Non-Technical Business Owners)
metaTitle: MCP Servers Explained: The 5 to Set Up First
metaDescription: MCP in plain English plus the 5 Model Context Protocol servers business owners should set up first: Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Notion, Slack.
excerpt: MCP is the USB-C for AI. Set up these 5 servers first and your AI can finally see your business: Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Notion, Slack.
author: Krista Mashore

featuredImage.alt: Five icons representing Gmail, Google Calendar, Google Drive, Notion, and Slack connected to a central AI brain

faq.question: Is MCP an Anthropic product?
faq.answer: MCP is an open standard. Anthropic created it and open-sourced it. From what I've seen with modelcontextprotocol.io, the protocol is supported by multiple AI clients including Claude and ChatGPT. Servers themselves can be built by anyone.

faq.question: Do I need a developer to set up MCP servers?
faq.answer: You don't need a developer, but you do need to follow setup instructions carefully. If editing a settings JSON file feels scary, ask a tech-friendly friend to sit with you for the first one.

faq.question: Are the Gmail / Slack / Notion MCP servers official?
faq.answer: Some are maintained by the apps themselves (for example, Notion has invested in MCP integration). Others are community projects. Always check permissions before installing and verify against the latest official list.

faq.question: What does this cost?
faq.answer: MCP is free. Most servers are free open-source projects. You pay for the AI models you use on top of what you already pay for Gmail, Notion, and Slack.

faq.question: Is my data safe?
faq.answer: Depends on the server. Self-hosted servers keep data local. Hosted servers may route through a third-party. Read the docs before trusting any server with your inbox or files.

faq.question: Why hasn't everyone done this already?
faq.answer: Because it sounds technical and most business owners stop reading at config file. The friction is just high enough to filter out almost everyone, which is exactly why it's a sleeper opportunity.

body:
You keep seeing the letters MCP and nodding like you know what they mean. You don't. That's fine, almost nobody does yet, which is why this whole topic is a sleeper opportunity for business owners willing to spend 90 minutes understanding it.

Here's what MCP is, in the plainest English I can manage. Then I'll tell you the 5 to set up first.

MCP in plain English

MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It's a standard, like USB-C, that lets AI tools (Claude, ChatGPT, your AI agents) plug into your stuff (Gmail, Calendar, Notion, Slack, etc.) without a custom integration for every single combination.

Before MCP, if you wanted Claude to read your Gmail, somebody had to write specific code connecting Claude to Gmail. Then specific code connecting Claude to Notion. Then specific code connecting ChatGPT to Gmail. And so on. A mess.

After MCP, you install one "Gmail MCP server." Any AI client that supports MCP (Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, others) can talk to it the same way. Build once, plug in everywhere.

From what I've seen with the official docs at modelcontextprotocol.io, think of it as a USB-C port for AI. That metaphor is correct, and I'm stealing it because it's the best one available.

Why this matters for your business right now

Because the people who set up their MCP servers in 2026 are going to look like wizards in 2027. Not because MCP is rocket science. Because everyone else is going to wait until it's mainstream, and by then the early adopters will have agents doing things they didn't even script.

The shift is this: instead of building a separate workflow for every task you want automated, you're going to talk to an AI that can already read your inbox, see your calendar, open your Drive, edit your Notion, and ping your team in Slack. You stop thinking in "automations." You start thinking in "tasks I want done."

That's the whole game.

The 5 MCP servers to set up first

I picked these 5 because they cover where most business owners actually live. Email, calendar, files, docs, team chat. If your AI assistant can touch these five, you're roughly most the way to an AI-run business.

1. Gmail (or Google Workspace email)

What it opens up: your assistant can read incoming mail, draft replies, search your inbox for old threads, label messages, and pull attachments. So instead of you forwarding things into ChatGPT, you say "Claude, what did Jen send me last week about the new offer, summarize her position and draft a reply." Done.

Setup: there are community-maintained Gmail MCP servers. Some require you to set up Google OAuth credentials yourself. Worth 45 minutes of one-time pain. If you use Claude Desktop, you'll add the server to your config file. Yes, you'll touch a JSON file once. You can do it.

Heads up: at the time I'm writing this, I'm not aware of an officially Anthropic-maintained Gmail server. Most options I've seen are community projects. Check their permissions before granting access, you want read scopes before write.

2. Google Calendar

What it opens up: your AI can see what you have today, find a free slot for a new prospect, reschedule conflicts when something blows up, and answer "when am I free next Tuesday after 3?" without you opening the app.

Setup: similar to Gmail. OAuth credentials. Add server to config. About 30 minutes once you've done Gmail.

This one alone saved me roughly 20 minutes a day. Not because finding a calendar slot takes long. Because the context switch of opening calendar, scanning, copy-pasting back to whoever asked, then switching back to what I was doing, that's the real time cost. The AI just answers.

3. Google Drive

What it opens up: your AI can search every document, slide deck, sheet, and PDF in your Drive. So when a client emails asking for "that pricing breakdown we sent in March," instead of you fumbling through Drive search, you ask Claude. It finds it. Draft the email with the file attached. Done in under a minute.

Setup: more OAuth. By the third one you're getting good at this.

Honest caveat. Drive can be slow if you have thousands of files. The MCP server has to search through them. Consider organizing into folders the AI can be pointed at, rather than turning it loose on your entire 12-year archive of junk.

4. Notion

What it opens up: this is where it gets fun. If you keep SOPs, client notes, project trackers, or your CRM in Notion, your AI can now read all of it and write to it. Add a row to your CRM database after every Zoom call. Update your project page with status notes. Pull up your service offer template, customize for a specific client, save the new version.

Setup: Notion has been one of the friendlier MCP integrations to set up. They've put effort into making it work cleanly. You generate an internal integration token in Notion settings, share the pages with the integration, point your MCP server at the token. Done in 15 minutes if you've done OAuth once.

Notion is the closest thing most small businesses have to an operating system. Plug your AI into it and you've got a brain that knows your business.

5. Slack

What it opens up: your AI can post to channels, summarize threads you missed, DM team members, react with emojis (yes, this matters). The killer use case for me: an agent that checks Slack every morning, summarizes overnight discussions in the team channels I'm in, and DMs me a one-paragraph rundown before I open the app.

Setup: Slack requires you to create a Slack App in their developer portal, install it to your workspace, generate a bot token, paste into the MCP server config. About 45 minutes the first time. There are written guides for the exact clicks.

A note on safety. Give the bot read-only permissions in week one. Watch what it does. Add write permissions (post messages, DM users) when you trust it. Don't grant admin scopes ever.

How to actually set these up without losing your weekend

Order I'd do it in:

  1. Install Claude Desktop (free download)
  2. Find a config file at the path Anthropic documents for your OS
  3. Set up Notion first because it's the friendliest
  4. Then Calendar (medium pain)
  5. Then Gmail (similar to Calendar)
  6. Then Drive (similar pattern)
  7. Then Slack last because the developer portal is the most complicated

Allow yourself a full Saturday. Yes, really. By Sunday you'll have an AI that can see your business.

The sleeper opportunity nobody's talking about

Most business owners I know are still copying things in and out of ChatGPT like it's 2024. Paste an email. Get a reply. Copy back. Paste into Gmail. Send. Five minutes per email. Ten emails a day. Almost an hour a day of context switching.

MCP kills that. Once your AI can see your inbox and your calendar and your docs natively, you stop being the API in your own business. The AI is.

This is the part where most articles would tell you everything's going to be easy. It won't. The first server you set up will be frustrating. You'll edit a JSON file wrong and Claude won't see the server. You'll grant the wrong OAuth scope. You'll get an authentication error you don't understand.

And then you'll fix it. And then you'll have something nobody on your block has, because nobody on your block is willing to spend a Saturday on this.

That's the opportunity.

Want to skip the trial and error?

Inside Level Up, we walk through exactly which MCP servers to set up for a real estate business, lender business, coaching business, or local service business. With the config templates, OAuth steps, and which scopes to grant. Built so you don't have to figure it out alone.

title: Email Nurture Sequences That Don't Sound Like AI: The Three-Pass Method
metaTitle: AI Email Nurture Sequences: Three-Pass Method
metaDescription: Why most AI email sequences read like spam, and the 3-pass workflow with Claude that fixes it. Structure, voice, and human details.
excerpt: Most AI email sequences sound like AI email sequences. Here's the three-pass method I use with Claude so my nurture emails read like a friend wrote them.
author: Krista Mashore

featuredImage.alt: Stylized email envelope with three colored stamps representing the three-pass writing method

faq.question: Why do AI emails sound so obvious?
faq.answer: Because the model is trained on a giant pool of marketing emails that all use the same hooks, the same transitions, and the same CTAs. Without intervention, it reaches for the average. Your job is to push it past the average.

faq.question: How long is a good nurture sequence?
faq.answer: Five to seven emails over 10 to 14 days for most coaching and real estate flows. Longer than that and people forget why they signed up. Shorter and you don't build enough trust.

faq.question: Should I write the brand voice prompt myself or have Claude help me build it?
faq.answer: Both. Start by giving Claude five emails you wrote by hand and asking it to describe your voice. Edit that description with your own words. Save it as a saved instruction or system prompt. That document becomes the spine of every pass.

faq.question: Does this work for cold outreach or just warm nurture?
faq.answer: It works for cold too, but the structure shifts. Cold needs more proof in the early emails and less storytelling. The three-pass method still applies, you just adjust what Pass 1 asks for.

faq.question: What's the biggest tell that an email was AI-written?
faq.answer: Symmetrical sentence rhythm. Every sentence the same length. Every paragraph perfectly balanced. Real human writing is lumpy. If your draft reads smooth like silk, break it up on purpose.

faq.question: Can I use ChatGPT instead of Claude for this?
faq.answer: You can. I prefer Claude for long-form voice work because it handles brand voice instructions more reliably in my experience. Use what you have and run the three passes.

body:
If you've ever opened an email from a coach and known within three seconds that ChatGPT wrote it, this article is for you.

I get those emails too. The opener says "I hope this email finds you well." There's a perfectly balanced three-bullet middle. The CTA is some variation of "click here to start your transformation today." Delete. Unsubscribe. Move on.

The problem isn't AI. The problem is the workflow most people use with AI. They write one prompt, take the output, and send it. That's like asking your assistant to draft an email and forwarding it without reading. Bad in 2018. Worse now that everybody can hear the AI fingerprint from a mile away.

Here's what I do instead. It's a three-pass method I built about eight months ago and refined across maybe 40 nurture sequences for my own list and for clients. It works because each pass does one job and one job only.

Why the obvious AI tells happen in the first place

Before I walk through the passes, you need to know why AI emails sound like AI emails. The model is trained on millions of marketing emails. Most of those emails are average. So when you ask Claude or any model to "write a nurture email," it reaches for the middle of the bell curve. That middle is generic.

The fix is not a better prompt. The fix is multiple prompts that each pull the email further away from the average.

Pass 1 handles structure. Pass 2 handles voice. Pass 3 handles the small specific human details that no model could invent because they happened to you.

Pass 1: Structure with Claude

Open Claude. Give it the topic, the audience, and the goal of the email. Tell it what came before in the sequence and what comes after. Don't ask for "good writing" yet. Ask for the bones.

My Pass 1 prompt looks something like this. "Write me a 220-word nurture email for an audience of new real estate agents who just signed up for my Level Up training. The goal is to get them to open email two, which teaches the first prospecting framework. Don't worry about voice yet. I want the skeleton. Strong opener for one person. A single teaching point. A soft CTA. Five short paragraphs."

The output is going to be flat. Don't panic. That's by design. You're going to layer voice on top in Pass 2.

What I do in Pass 1: check the structure makes sense, confirm the teaching point is one point and not three, make sure the CTA isn't trying to do two jobs.

What I don't do: rewrite a single sentence yet.

Pass 2: Voice rewrite

This is where most people skip. They take the Pass 1 output and call it done. Don't.

For Pass 2, I have a saved Claude project with my brand voice in the instructions. It's about 600 words of voice rules. Things like "never use em-dashes," "contractions are required," "talk to one person not a crowd," "no corporate filler words." Plus three or four example emails I wrote by hand that captured my voice well.

I drop the Pass 1 draft into that project and say "rewrite this in my voice. Don't change the structure or the teaching point. Just the language. Match the rhythm of my example emails. Use short sentences mixed with longer ones."

The output of Pass 2 is dramatically better than Pass 1. It sounds like me in the broad strokes. The cadence is right. The word choices are closer to mine.

But it's still not done.

Pass 3: Tighten with specific human details

This is the pass that separates "sounds like a person wrote this" from "sounds like Krista wrote this." It's the part I do by hand. No prompt. Just me reading the draft and adding the things that only happen in real life.

Here are the kinds of things I add in Pass 3:

  • A reference to the weather or the time of day I'm writing
  • The name of the actual coffee shop I'm sitting in (I do this maybe some of the time)
  • A self-correction mid-thought ("scratch that, the better example is...")
  • A reference to a specific student or call from earlier in the week
  • A piece of mild irritation about something that happened that morning
  • A casual aside in parentheses that adds nothing informational but feels human

I'm not adding fluff. I'm adding fingerprints. AI can mimic voice. It cannot mimic your Tuesday morning. That's why Pass 3 is the load-bearing wall.

The before-and-after test

Quick gut check. After Pass 3, I read the email out loud. Three things must be true:

  • I can hear my own voice in my head when I read it
  • There's at least one sentence I would never trust an assistant to write for me
  • The CTA feels like a friend pointing at something useful, not a closer pushing a deal

If any of those three fail, I do another small Pass 3. Usually two minutes of work.

A real example of the three passes (compressed)

Topic: new agents struggling with prospecting consistency.

Pass 1 (structure, Claude raw): "Consistency is the foundation of every successful prospecting habit. Many agents start strong and lose momentum. The solution is a simple daily framework. Here are three steps you can take..."

That's fine. It's also forgettable.

Pass 2 (voice): "Most agents start prospecting like they're going to win the championship in 30 days. They burn out by week three. The agents I see building a real book of business are the ones who do something boring every day instead of something amazing once a month."

Better. The voice is closer. But still a touch too clean.

Pass 3 (human detail, by hand): "Most agents start prospecting like they're going to win the championship in 30 days. They burn out by week three. (I did this for years. I'd make 80 calls on a Monday and zero calls all of Wednesday.) The agents I see building a real book of business now? They do something boring every day instead of something amazing once a month."

The parenthetical is the fingerprint. The model could not have written that line because it didn't happen to the model. It happened to me.

What this means for your sequence

You don't need fancier prompts. You need a workflow with passes. Pass 1 for shape. Pass 2 for voice. Pass 3 for the human dust.

A five-email sequence used to take me a full day to write by hand. Now I can run a full sequence in about 90 minutes using this method, and the open rates are higher than my hand-written era. Not because AI is better than me. Because the workflow lets me focus my human energy where it matters: the small specific details no model can fake.

Want to see the saved Claude project I use?

I share my brand voice setup, the actual prompts for each pass, and three full nurture sequences inside Level Up. We walk through them together so you can see how the voice prompt is written and how Pass 3 changes a flat draft into something that pulls replies. Come grab a seat at Level Up and I'll hand you the templates.

title: Claude vs. ChatGPT for a Non-Technical Business Owner in 2026
metaTitle: Claude vs. ChatGPT for Business Owners (2026)
metaDescription: An honest comparison of Claude and ChatGPT for non-technical business owners in 2026. Where each one wins, where it loses, and which one to pick.
excerpt: An honest, opinionated comparison of Claude and ChatGPT for a non-technical business owner in 2026. Pick one, master it, stop paying for both.
author: Krista Mashore

featuredImage.alt: Claude vs ChatGPT comparison for non-technical business owners in 2026

faq.question: Which one has a better free tier in 2026?
faq.answer: Both offer free tiers with meaningful limits. The free version of either is fine for trying the tool, neither is enough for serious daily business work. Verify what each free tier includes today on the official sites.

faq.question: Can I export my data if I switch?
faq.answer: Both platforms offer conversation export. The format and completeness of the export has varied. If portability matters to you, test the export feature before you build a lot of work inside one platform.

faq.question: What about Gemini, Perplexity, and the others?
faq.answer: For business owners who want one writing-and-thinking tool, Claude and ChatGPT are still the two main contenders. Perplexity is search-first, Gemini is strong inside Google Workspace, but for deep-work writing the Claude vs ChatGPT comparison is the one that matters.

faq.question: Is Claude safer to use for client data?
faq.answer: Both Anthropic and OpenAI publish data usage policies for their consumer and business plans. Anthropic has been vocal about not training on consumer-plan inputs by default. OpenAI's policies have shifted by plan tier. Read both current policies if client data privacy is a real constraint for you.

faq.question: If I already pay for ChatGPT, is it worth adding Claude?
faq.answer: For 60 to 90 days, yes, to see if Claude's writing quality lands for you the way it has for most of my coaching clients. After that trial, pick one. Stop paying for both unless you have a clear use case for each.

faq.question: Will this comparison still be true in 6 months?
faq.answer: Probably mostly. Definitely not entirely. Both companies ship aggressively. The strategic shapes have held for a while and will probably continue. The specific feature winners on any given week will shift. Pick on the shape, not the week.

body:
You don't care which AI is technically better at a benchmark. You care which one is going to save you 8 hours a week and not make your content sound like a real estate brochure from 2014. That's the actual question. I get it asked roughly four times a week, usually by a solopreneur who already pays for both and feels guilty about not using either of them well. So let me give you the honest comparison, from someone who's been running both for years.

A note up front: pricing and features shift constantly on both sides. I'll point at the rough lay of the land as of May 2026 and tell you what I'd verify on each company's site before you buy.

The short version

For a non-technical business owner who writes a lot, my pick is Claude. For a business owner who depends heavily on images, voice mode, or the ChatGPT plugins stack, ChatGPT is still the right call. If you want to pick only one, which is what I recommend, Claude wins for most coaches, consultants, agents, and lenders. If you want the longer version of why, keep reading.

Where Claude wins

Writing voice

This is the one that matters most for service-based businesses and it's not close. Claude writes copy that sounds more like a person and less like a press release. ChatGPT can write in a custom voice if you push hard, but the gravitational pull back to corporate phrasing is stronger. Across hundreds of side-by-side tests I've done with coaching clients, Claude's first draft is closer to publishable about a lot of the time.

This is also why Anthropic's Skills feature matters so much for business owners. A brand voice Skill, paired with Claude's natural writing strength, gets you to "this sounds like me" with way less friction than the equivalent setup in ChatGPT.

Long context

Claude has been the longer-context model for most of its life and continues to be a strong choice for working with big documents. If you regularly paste in a 40-page transcript, a long sales page, a book chapter, or a stack of client emails and ask Claude to work with all of it, Claude will hold more of it in working memory and stay coherent further into the document. Both models have improved here, but Claude's lead on long-document work has been consistent enough that I default to Claude for any task involving more than 5,000 words of input.

Projects + Skills as a system

This is the underrated part. Claude's combination of Projects (workspaces with persistent files and instructions) and Skills (reusable instruction packages that load on demand) gives you a layered authoring system that ChatGPT's custom GPTs don't quite match for the business-owner use case. Custom GPTs are great for distinct one-off tools you want to share. Projects + Skills are great for building a personal AI operating system around your business. Different design intent, different winner depending on what you're trying to do.

Claude Code and Claude Desktop

If you're starting to dip into automation, Claude Code (Anthropic's command-line and editor-integrated coding agent) and Claude Desktop give you a path from "I write things" to "I automate things" without leaving the Anthropic stack. For a non-technical owner, this matters less on day one. On month six, when you're trying to wire up an MCP server to your calendar or your CRM, it matters a lot.

Where ChatGPT wins

I'm not in the business of bashing tools I use. Here are the places ChatGPT genuinely beats Claude for a business owner.

Image generation

This isn't even a fight. ChatGPT's image generation has been the default for most people for a while, and the integration inside the chat is smooth. If your business depends on quick custom graphics, mockups, illustrations, or visual variations of a concept, ChatGPT is the better daily driver. Claude has been adding capabilities here but it's not the leader.

Voice mode

ChatGPT's voice mode has been ahead of Claude's audio capabilities for most of the last two years. If you're someone who talks to your AI while walking the dog or driving, ChatGPT remains the better experience. I know multiple coaches who do their best thinking out loud and ChatGPT voice has become their daily creative partner. Claude's surfaces are catching up, but it's not where I'd send a voice-first user yet.

Custom GPTs stack

OpenAI built a real community around custom GPTs. If you want to grab a pre-built GPT for a specific niche task (a tax helper, a niche SEO tool, a meme generator) the ChatGPT store has more options than the equivalent third-party Claude stack. For some business owners this is a deal-maker. For most coaches and consultants, the depth of the stack matters less than the depth of one well-built Project, but I won't pretend the stack isn't bigger on the OpenAI side.

Pricing as of now (verify before you buy)

Both companies publish current pricing on their sites and both have changed it more than once in the past 18 months. Rough shape as of May 2026:

Claude Pro and Claude Max are the two main consumer-paid tiers from Anthropic, with Claude Team as the small-business shared option. Higher tiers get higher usage limits, more access to top models, and Skills and Projects features included.

ChatGPT Plus, ChatGPT Pro (the higher-cost personal tier), and ChatGPT Team are the equivalents on the OpenAI side. Higher tiers get more usage, access to advanced models, and team workspace features.

The actual dollar figures move. I'm not quoting them here because if I quote a number today and OpenAI or Anthropic shifts pricing next week, this article becomes wrong. Go to anthropic.com/pricing and openai.com/chatgpt/pricing directly. Compare the two pages on the same day. Pick.

My honest recommendation: pick one, master it

The biggest mistake I see business owners make is paying for both, half-using both, and never getting really good at either. You don't need both. You need one really well set up.

For a business owner who writes a lot, who cares about voice, who works with longer documents, and who wants to build an AI system around their business, my answer is Claude. Set up Projects, build a voice Skill, get to the point where Claude sounds like you. That's worth ten times more than dabbling in both tools.

If you generate a lot of images, work mostly through voice, or need the breadth of the GPT marketplace, ChatGPT is the right pick. Same advice on the other side: go deep, build a custom GPT or two that match your business, and stop tab-hopping.

Pattern interrupt: I actually still keep ChatGPT around as a backup. I use it maybe twice a month, almost always for an image or for a quick second opinion when Claude is being too polite. But most my real work lives in Claude. If you'd asked me three years ago I'd have given you the opposite answer. Tools change. Be willing to switch when the math changes.

If you want my full AI stack for a service business, including which model I use for what, how my Projects are structured, and the voice Skill template I give my coaching clients, that's the system I teach inside Level Up.

title: Claude Skills: Build Your Brand Voice Skill Once and Reuse Forever
metaTitle: Claude Skills: Build a Brand Voice Skill Once, Reuse Forever
metaDescription: How to build your first Claude Skill. The brand voice Skill, step by step, with a real example output.
excerpt: Anthropic introduced Claude Skills in 2025 and they're the missing layer most business owners don't know exists. Here's how to build your brand voice Skill first.
author: Krista Mashore

featuredImage.alt: Claude Skills brand voice walkthrough for coaches and consultants

faq.question: Where do I actually create a Skill?
faq.answer: The exact creation flow depends on the Claude surface you use (Claude.ai, Claude Desktop, Claude Code, or the API). Anthropic's documentation has the current step-by-step. Check there because the UI has shifted a couple of times since launch.

faq.question: Are Skills available on the free plan?
faq.answer: Skills availability has varied by plan since launch. Verify current plan inclusions on Anthropic's pricing page before assuming. If you're a business owner you want a paid plan anyway.

faq.question: Can I share a Skill with my team?
faq.answer: Yes, on team-level plans. Sharing across personal accounts has been more limited. Check current sharing options if this is critical to your setup.

faq.question: Can a Skill override a Project's instructions?
faq.answer: Skills and Project instructions work together. The Project sets persistent context, the Skill adds specialized behavior on demand. They don't usually conflict if you've written both cleanly. If they do, the Skill's specificity tends to win in the moments it's active.

faq.question: How long should a voice Skill be?
faq.answer: Most of mine are between 1,500 and 3,000 words including examples. Long enough to be specific, short enough that Claude can actually hold it. The examples eat most of the word count and that's correct.

faq.question: What's the next Skill to build after voice?
faq.answer: Whatever output you create most often. For most coaches and consultants that's either an email Skill or a long-form content Skill. Build the one that saves you the most weekly hours first.

body:
You finally got Claude sounding like you in one chat. The cadence was right, the words were yours, you stopped flinching when you read the output back. And then you opened a new conversation the next day and Claude was back to writing like a LinkedIn coach with a thesaurus. I know the feeling. The fix is a Claude Skill, and once you build one for your brand voice, that one specific problem becomes solved forever across every conversation, every Project, every team member you bring in.

Skills are the thing nobody is talking about loud enough yet, and they should be. Let me walk you through what they are, and then how to build the single most valuable one for your business: your voice.

What a Claude Skill actually is

Anthropic introduced Skills in 2025 as a way to package up reusable instructions, examples, and reference files that Claude can load on demand inside a conversation. Think of it as a kind of recipe folder. You write the recipe once. You name it. Then when you (or your team) need that specific output, Claude knows to pull that recipe in and follow it.

The mechanics: a Skill is a folder with a description file (usually a SKILL.md or skill.md) that tells Claude when and how to apply it, plus any supporting files you want it to reference. Claude reads the description, decides whether the Skill is relevant, and loads it into context when it is. You don't have to remember to invoke it. The platform does the matching for you.

This is a different thing from a Project. A Project is a workspace with persistent files and instructions for a specific scope of work (your business, a particular client, a particular product line). A Skill is portable. You can use the same Skill across multiple Projects, share it with teammates, or upgrade it once and have every workflow that depends on it improve automatically. Voice rules belong in a Skill. Knowledge documents belong in a Project. Both layers matter.

The exact way you create and manage Skills depends on whether you're using Claude.ai, Claude Desktop, or Claude Code, and Anthropic has been shipping updates to this regularly. So I'll keep the steps tool-agnostic and you can apply them in whichever surface you use.

Why brand voice is the first Skill to build

Most business owners try to build their first Skill around a workflow, like an email sequence generator or a sales page outliner. That's a mistake. Workflow Skills are useful, but they'll only ever be as good as the voice they output in. If the voice is generic, the workflow Skill produces generic copy 50 times faster. That's worse than slow generic copy. That's scale on a broken foundation.

Voice is the foundation. Build it first. Once you have a voice Skill that genuinely sounds like you, every subsequent Skill (your email Skill, your social Skill, your podcast outline Skill) inherits the voice automatically and you stop fighting your AI on the same problem 200 times.

Step 1: Extract your voice

You can't write a voice Skill from memory. You have to extract it from actual writing you've done that you're proud of. Here's the process I use, which usually takes me about 2 hours one afternoon.

Pull together 8 to 12 pieces of your own writing. Emails, social posts, sales pages, podcast intros, anything where the voice was unmistakably you and the response from your audience was strong. Paste them into a single document.

Now open a Claude conversation and ask Claude to act as a voice analyst. Prompt it with something close to this: "Below are 10 samples of my writing. Analyze them and extract a voice profile. I want: sentence rhythm patterns, recurring words I actually use, words I appear to deliberately avoid, formatting tendencies, common rhetorical moves, and 3 to 5 example sentences that capture the voice at its strongest. Be specific. Don't give me adjectives."

Claude will give you back a draft voice profile. Read it. Some of it will be accurate, some will be off. Edit it. The edited version becomes the heart of your Skill.

Step 2: Structure the Skill

A good voice Skill has roughly six sections:

A short description at the top that tells Claude when to apply it. Something like "Apply this Skill any time you are writing copy, social posts, emails, or any content that goes out to my audience." This is the part Claude reads to decide whether to load the Skill.

A "voice in one paragraph" summary so a new collaborator (or a new Claude conversation) can get oriented fast.

A "use these words" list. Specific words you actually use. Not adjectives like "warm" but the literal words like "agents," "lenders," "systems," "stack."

A "never use these words" list. The banned terms. For me that's the entire corporate vocabulary plus the AI tells: use, use, dive, dig in, work through, path, open up, solid. Put yours in writing.

A "patterns to avoid" section. The triplet pattern, the question-then-list-answer pattern, the suspiciously perfect transition, the "it's not X, it's Y" formulation. If you don't put these in writing, Claude will drift back to them every time.

A "examples" section with 4 to 8 short passages of your actual writing. These are the highest signal part of the Skill. Claude pattern-matches against examples more strongly than against rules.

Save it. Name it something obvious like "krista-voice" or "[yourname]-voice." Make sure the description field is clear enough that Claude will know when to trigger it.

Step 3: Test it (this is where most people stop too soon)

Spin up a fresh conversation. Don't give Claude any other context, just trigger the Skill or upload it depending on your tool. Then ask Claude to write a piece of content you'd normally write yourself.

Read the output out loud. Yes really. Out loud. If it sounds like you ranting in your kitchen, congrats. If it sounds like a thoughtful corporate cousin of you, the Skill isn't done yet.

Common gaps I see on first drafts: not enough banned words listed (the AI tells slip back in), examples that are too short to anchor rhythm, rules that contradict each other so Claude picks the safer/blander rule. Go fix the Skill, test again. Three rounds and you'll have something that holds.

Pattern interrupt: my first voice Skill took me four attempts before it sounded right. The fourth attempt was different because I stopped trying to describe my voice and just dumped in 6 full paragraphs of stuff I'd actually written. The examples did most the work. So if you're stuck, add more examples.

A real example of the difference

Same prompt, "Write a 150-word LinkedIn post about why most agents fail at consistent content."

Without the voice Skill, Claude wrote something starting with "In today's competitive real estate landscape, content consistency separates thriving agents from those struggling to grow." That sentence alone has three of my banned phrases.

With the voice Skill loaded, Claude wrote, "Most agents don't fail at content. They fail at the 90 seconds before the content, which is the part where they decide what they actually think. Then they sit in front of a phone with nothing to say and call it writer's block." That second one I'd actually post. The first one I'd delete.

That gap is the gap a voice Skill closes.

If you want the full voice extraction template I use with my coaching clients, plus the rest of the AI authority system that sits around it, that's all inside Level Up. I built it specifically for coaches and consultants who need their AI to sound like them, not like everyone else's AI.

title: Brand Voice Fingerprinting: How to Capture How You Write So Claude Sounds Like You
metaTitle: Brand Voice Fingerprinting: Make Claude Sound Like You
metaDescription: Step-by-step process to extract your writing voice into a reusable Claude Skill or Project so every AI draft sounds like you, not corporate beige.
excerpt: A 5-step process to extract your writing voice into a reusable Claude Skill or Project. Stop sounding like a LinkedIn carousel had a baby with a corporate apology.
author: Krista Mashore

featuredImage.alt: Diagram showing the brand voice fingerprinting process: raw writing samples flowing into a Claude Skill that produces on-brand AI drafts

faq.question: Do I need a Claude Skill, or is a Project enough?
faq.answer: For most solo entrepreneurs, a Project is enough to start. Add a Skill when you want the same voice to work across multiple Projects or when you want to share it with team members.

faq.question: How many writing samples do I need?
faq.answer: Thirty minimum. Sixty is better. Three is a coincidence, thirty is a pattern.

faq.question: What if my voice has changed over the years?
faq.answer: Use samples from the last 12-18 months only. Older samples will confuse the pattern. You can fingerprint different eras of your voice if you want, but most people don't need to.

faq.question: Does this work for video and audio scripts too?
faq.answer: Yes, and it works better than for written content because spoken patterns are usually more distinctive. Feed in transcripts of you talking, not polished scripts.

faq.question: How do I know if my fingerprint is too vague?
faq.answer: If the document could describe more than one person you follow, it's too vague. A real fingerprint should feel uncomfortably specific to you, almost like reading your diary.

faq.question: Can I drop a few examples into Claude and skip the fingerprint?
faq.answer: You can, and it'll work okay for a single piece. But you'll be feeding the same examples into every new chat forever. A fingerprint plus a Skill or Project means you set it up once and reuse it everywhere.

body:
You read your own AI-written email out loud yesterday, didn't you. And about three sentences in you cringed because it didn't sound like you, it sounded like a LinkedIn carousel had a baby with a corporate apology letter. Don't worry. You're in the right place.

The reason almost every AI output sounds like that is not because the AI is broken. It's because nobody has bothered to capture how you write. Not adjectives. Not vibes. The patterns, sentence shapes, word choices, and weird little habits that make your writing recognizable. This process has a name and I call it brand voice fingerprinting. It's the single highest-impact thing you can do with AI right now, and most people skip it.

Here's how I do it. Plus how I package it into a reusable Claude Skill that I can drop into any project.

What "voice fingerprinting" means

A fingerprint is a unique pattern. Yours is different from mine. Same with how you write. You probably:

  • start more sentences with "And" or "So" than most people
  • avoid certain words on purpose (mine: that corporate L-word that means "use" makes me twitch)
  • have a few signature phrases you've been using for years without noticing
  • have a sentence-length pattern (mine is short, then long, then medium)
  • show up differently in emails than in social posts
  • contradict yourself on purpose sometimes
  • use parentheses for personality, not only clarification

Voice fingerprinting is the process of cataloguing all of that and turning it into instructions an AI can follow. Then packaging those instructions so you don't have to retype them into every new chat.

Step 1: Pull your raw material

Open a folder somewhere. Could be Google Drive, could be Notion, could be a single Word doc.

Now collect at least 30 samples of your actual writing. I said 30. Not three.

Mix it up. Pull from:

  • The last 10 emails you wrote to clients (skip the boilerplate ones)
  • Five social posts you wrote yourself (not the ones a VA wrote)
  • Three sales pages or long-form pieces
  • Five voice memos transcribed into text (this one's underrated, the way you talk leaks into how you should write)
  • Five short replies you sent in DMs or Slack
  • Anything else that feels like "yeah, that's me"

The reason you need this much material is that AI is a pattern-matching machine. Patterns need data. Three samples isn't a pattern, it's a coincidence.

Step 2: Run the extraction prompt

This is where the magic happens, and it's a long prompt, so plan for it.

Open Claude. (You can do this in ChatGPT too. The output is roughly the same. I prefer Claude for this particular task because in my testing it's been better at picking up on stylistic quirks instead of summarizing tone, but your mileage may vary.)

Paste all 30 samples in. Then ask:

Analyze these writing samples and produce a voice fingerprint document. Include: signature words and phrases this person uses repeatedly. Words they appear to avoid.
